content.description:
Hobson uses an event system for reactive programming. Code can subscribe to events and respond when things happen in the system.

## Event Types

Events are organized into namespaces:

### Item Events (`item:*`)

| Event | When Fired | Payload |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| `item:created` | New item saved | `{ item }` |
| `item:updated` | Existing item modified | `{ item, previousItem }` |
| `item:deleted` | Item removed | `{ itemId }` |

### System Events (`system:*`)

| Event | When Fired | Payload |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| `system:boot-complete` | Kernel finished booting | `{}` |
| `system:error` | Unhandled error occurred | `{ error, context, timestamp }` |

### Selection Events (`selection:*`)

| Event | When Fired | Payload |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| `selection:changed` | Selected item changed | `{ itemId, previousItemId }` |

### Viewport Events (`viewport:*`)

| Event | When Fired | Payload |
|-------|-----------|--------|
| `viewport:root-changed` | Navigation occurred | `{ rootId, previousRootId }` |

## Subscribing to Events

### In Code (Runtime)

```javascript
// Subscribe
const unsubscribe = api.events.on('item:updated', (event) => {
  console.log('Item changed:', event.item.id);
});

// Wildcard subscription
api.events.on('item:*', (event) => {
  console.log('Some item event:', event);
});

// Later: unsubscribe
unsubscribe();
```

### Declarative Watches

Code items can declare event subscriptions that activate automatically:

```javascript
{
  type: "library-uuid",
  content: {
    watches: [
      { event: "item:created", type: "note-type-uuid" },
      { event: "item:updated", typeExtends: "code-type-uuid" },
      { event: "system:error" }
    ],
    code: `
      export async function onItemCreated(event, api) {
        // Called when a note is created
      }

      export async function onItemUpdated(event, api) {
        // Called when any code item is updated
      }

      export async function onSystemError(event, api) {
        // Called on any system error
      }
    `
  }
}
```

### Watch Filters

| Filter | Meaning |
|--------|--------|
| `type` | Only items of exactly this type |
| `typeExtends` | Items whose type chain includes this type |
| (none) | All events of this name |

### Handler Naming Convention

The handler function name is derived from the event name:

| Event | Handler |
|-------|--------|
| `item:created` | `onItemCreated` |
| `item:updated` | `onItemUpdated` |
| `system:error` | `onSystemError` |
| `selection:changed` | `onSelectionChanged` |

## Event Flow Example

When a note is saved:

```
1. api.set(noteItem) called
2. Storage validates and writes to IndexedDB
3. Kernel emits item:created (or item:updated)
4. EventBus notifies all subscribers:
   - Runtime subscriptions (api.events.on)
   - Declarative watches matching the event
5. Handlers execute with event data and API access
```

## Built-in Event Handlers

| Handler | Watches | Purpose |
|---------|---------|--------|
| [default-error-handler](item://e7707000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002) | `system:error` | Creates error items, shows toast |
| [view-update-watcher](item://dafc22a5-13bf-4b42-937d-52f002c4bc70) | `item:updated` (views) | Re-renders when view code changes |

## Creating Event-Driven Extensions

1. Create a library item
2. Add `content.watches` array
3. Export handler functions matching the naming convention
4. The kernel auto-wires them on boot

This is how the error handling system works—no kernel modification needed.

## Watch Filter Evaluation

Filters are evaluated at **event-fire time**, not cached at boot:

1. When an event fires, the kernel queries all code items with matching `watches`
2. For each watcher, filters are evaluated against the event's item:
   - `type`: exact match `event.item.type === filter.type`
   - `typeExtends`: walks the item's type chain, checks if `filter.typeExtends` is in the chain
   - `id`: exact match `event.item.id === filter.id`
3. Multiple filter fields are AND-ed together
4. No filters (only `event`) matches all events of that type

**Why event-fire time?** Type chains can change dynamically. Caching at boot would miss new types.

## Design Rationale

### Why Declarative Over Imperative?

Imperative `api.events.on()` requires code to run at boot time to register listeners. Declarative watches as data:
- Enable "what watches what" queries without executing code
- Automatic lifecycle management (no manual subscribe/unsubscribe)
- Fit the "everything is data" philosophy
- Are inspectable — you can query which items watch which events

### Why Automatic Subscriptions?

When a renderer renders an item, the kernel automatically subscribes to changes in that item's attachments. This:
- Eliminates boilerplate subscription management
- Ensures consistency (can't forget to subscribe)
- Limits notifications to direct parents only (prevents cascading re-renders)

## See Also

- [kernel:core](item://33333333-1111-0000-0000-000000000000) — EventBus implementation
- [default-error-handler](item://e7707000-0000-0000-0000-000000000002) — Example event handler
- [Error Handling](item://c0c0c0c0-0023-0000-0000-000000000000) — Three-tier error system using watches
- [concept:kernel](item://c0c0c0c0-0001-0000-0000-000000000000) — What's kernel vs userland
- [concept:repl](item://c0c0c0c0-0008-0000-0000-000000000000) — Testing events via `api.events`
